paragraphs.text: THE INTELLECTUAL ASPECT the sensual. Using this division, we will begin looking at each aspect.
The Intellectual Aspect. If there is confusion within us, it has its greatest scope within our thoughts; our very desires and feelings are already stirred up under the action of the thoughts.

In the category of thought, however, not everything is chaotic motion; in the midst of these thoughts are a number ofsubstantial operations. It is precisely these that comprise actual mental life, from the standpoint of the thoughts. T hese operations are as follows: 1) As soon as you have observed something in me by means of your senses, or you have heard others relate how they have observed something with their senses, at that instant the imagination is imagining, and the memory is remembering, everything; nothing can enter the soul without the imagination and memory. Thus, subsequent mental activity rests upon the imagination and the memory. If something is not stored in the memory, you will not be able to imagine it, let alone think about it. It does happen that thoughts are sometimes born directly from the soul, but then they immediately assume a form. Thus, the entire intellectual aspect of the soul is imaginative. 2) The imagination and memory obtain and store only material for thoughts. The motion of thought itself comes out of the soul and operates according to the laws of the soul. Do you remember how your youngest brother, once he had seen something new, would immediately ask you or someone else, “What is that? Who made that? What is it made of?” He would not calm down until all of these questions were answered to his satisfaction. The intellection of the soul begins specifically as the result of these questions, and it bears thoughts in answer to them or it receives ready-made answers from others. The imagination and the memory do not think. They are the unskilled labor force, the backbone. The faculty of the soul, from which 49

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE arise such questions and thoughts that are sought and born in answer to them, is called the intellect^ whose business it is to reason, think things over, and reach the necessary conclusions.

Observe yourself, and you will find that you cannot do anything without thinking it over. Every trifle must be deliberated. No matter how quickly this is accomplished, the understanding enters into everything and goes by the aforementioned questions. 3) While you are thinking something over, there is as yet no clearly defined thought. A definite thought comes into being when you find an answer to one of the questions. Your intellect sorts through everything, seeking to find what kind of thing something is, where it comes from, what it is for, and the like.

When you find an answer yourself, or you have heard it from someone else and agree with it, you usually say. Now I understand, I do not need any further explanation, the matter is resolved.” This answer quiets your intellection with respect to the subject you have been studying. Then your intellect turns to other subjects, while the thought which has just been formed is given over to the mental archive—the memory, from which, by the demand of necessity, it is pulled as an aid in the answering of other questions, and as a means for composing other thoughts. The sum total of concepts that have been formed in this way constitutes the image of your thoughts, which image you also detect whenever you are speaking. This is the realm of the knowledge you obtain through mental labor. The more problems you solve, the more definite thoughts or ideas about things you will have; the more such ideas you have, the broader the sphere of your knowledge. In this way, as you see, your intellect stands above the memory and imagination; this intellect, along with intellectual labor, obtains for you definite concepts or cognitions about things.
